story:
My friends call me ILII—infinitely large, infinitely intelligent. I also have ITD—inability to die. It’s genetic, incurable, and a terrible cure for boredom. I’ve mastered every profession, gone pro in every sport, tried every hobby for millennia at a time. I even tested out villainy, did eons in prison, and sat through countless botched executions. None of it stuck. Boredom did.

One night, during my 1,256,257th attempt to die of boredom, I found an old bottle of bubble solution in the basement and blew a bubble. Done it before. This time, though, hard formations began to clump inside, and some got coated in a thin film of organic material. I showed my friends. “Enter it in the fair,” they said. Fine. Why not?

Of course, fairs come with someone who has better stuff. This other ILII—no ITD, lucky him—brought a bubble thick with hard structures and organic film that could actually think. Not all of it, but enough. One thinking form even deduced that the bubble had once been unimaginably tiny and gave that origin a flashy name: the Big Bang. The owner took this personally, as if the bubble had insulted him, and decided he’d destroy it. He still won first prize.

The funny part? He didn’t stop there. He struts around puffing the same bubble larger and larger, as if expansion is a personality trait. I told him to stop—bubbles pop—but he shrugged. “I can always blow a new one.” What a jerk.

Meanwhile, I’m waiting for my own organic film to do something truly interesting. I can wait. Time is the only thing I have too much of. But watching him parade his swelling bubble started grinding on me. The more he blew, the thinner that film stretched, the closer the pop. Not that I care about the little thought-makers in there, but I’m curious. They’ve already figured out beginnings. What else might they reach? How far could they go if someone would just stop inflating the walls around them?

So an idea took shape—ugly, yes, but boredom breeds uglier. I caught myself planning to take him out and take the bubble. Two birds, one stone: I’d get more prison time (underrated as a change of scenery), maybe another theatrical, inevitably failed execution. More importantly, I’d stop the blowing. No more reckless expansion. Let the thinkers keep thinking. Let them chase every last puzzle until there’s nothing left to figure out. Only then—only when the story is complete—would I let the bubble go.

To be clear, plotting harm is wrong, and I know it. Even I can hear the echo of my own pettiness. But immortality dulls the edges of right and wrong; everything smears into “what passes the time.” I’m not pretending this is noble. It’s curiosity mixed with spite, with a dash of professional interest. The other ILII calls his bubble replaceable. I don’t believe him. You don’t get many worlds where the film wakes up and names its own beginning.

So I wait, watching my bubble for a spark and his for a crack, undecided about how far I’ll go, certain only of this: if the thinking film inside his bubble can reach the end of the map, I want to be there when it does.
